help_subtitle: SelfCare, talkgroups, OPTIONS, Talker Alias, and hotspot setup — frequently asked questions.
help_guide_desc: Step-by-step instructions on adn.systems. Video tutorial (Spanish) from NetDigital Venezuela.
help_step1_title: Click on the "Selfcare" menu on the top navigation bar.
help_img_step1: Selfcare menu and Access / Set / Recover Password button
help_step2_title: Click on the button "Access / Set / Recover Password".
help_img_step2: Form: callsign, email, Verify Identity button
help_step3_title: Enter your callsign and email address registered on RadioID.net, and click "Verify Identity". You will receive a link in your email to access the selfcare panel.
help_step3_caption: You will receive a link in your email. In the selfcare panel:
help_step4_title: If you have more than one ID registered to your callsign, select the ID for which you want to set the hotspot password.
help_step4_email: You will receive a confirmation email with the new password you must use to connect your hotspots.
help_step5_title: Configure your hotspot to use your new password
help_step5a_li4: Type your new password in the "Password" field.
help_step5a_li6: Reboot hotspot if connection does not work.
help_step5b_li2: Admin → Advanced → Quick Editors → DMR → DMR Gateway.
help_step5b_li4: Type your new password in the "Password" field.
help_step5b_li6: Reboot hotspot if connection does not work.
help_img_pista: Password field in DMR Network (Pi-Star / WPSD)
help_step_done: Your new secure password will be ready to connect successfully to the ADN Systems DMR network.
help_faq0_a: ADN SelfCare lets you create and manage your "DMR-ID secure" password. This password protects your DMR ID when connecting to ADN Systems (repeaters, hotspots, bridges). Use the SelfCare link to set it.
help_faq4_q: Is the DMR-ID secure password optional?
help_faq4_a1: Yes. It is optional. Users can choose whether to create it or not. If you do not create it, nothing changes. If you do create it, you must then use it in every client or device you use to connect to ADN Systems.
help_faq5_q: Where do I need to enter the password?
help_faq5_a1: If you create a DMR-ID secure password, you must enter it in all the software or devices you use to connect to ADN Systems. For example:
help_faq5_a2: At present, many of these programs do not yet have a dedicated field for this password. However, there are ways to enter it in most of them. Efforts are being made so that in the near future this option is visible in the main clients.
help_faq6_q: My client has no field for the password. What can I do?
help_faq6_a1: Many programs do not yet have a dedicated field for the DMR-ID secure password. There are workarounds to enter it in most of them. Efforts are underway so that in the near future this option is visible in the main clients.
help_ss_options_q: Self Service and the OPTIONS line
help_options_format_intro: The OPTIONS line is a semicolon-separated list of KEY=value pairs (Homebrew format). The server stores it for your hotspot and applies it on registration.
help_options_format_routing: You can edit this line in Self Service (if your network enables it) or paste it directly into your hotspot software — see the next sections.
help_ss_configure_steps_title: Edit and save in Self Service
help_ss_configure_li1: Select your hotspot DMR ID if your callsign has more than one registered ID.
help_ss_configure_li2: Build the OPTIONS line with the embedded calculator or edit the text field directly.
help_ss_configure_li3: Save. The ADN server updates your hotspot registration; changes apply on the next RPTO from the device.
help_ss_options_a1: If your network enables it, the Self Service page lets you log in and edit your hotspot preferences without asking a sysop. Changes are stored as an OPTIONS line and pushed to the ADN server, which updates your hotspot registration.
help_ss_options_li1: TS1= and TS2= — comma-separated static talkgroup numbers for each timeslot.
help_ss_options_li2: VOICE= and LANG= — voice ident on/off and announcement language.
help_ss_options_li3: SINGLE= and TIMER= — how dynamic talkgroups behave (see below).
help_ss_options_li4: The line must end with a semicolon (;).
help_ss_options_calc: Use the Options Calculator in the menu to build the line, then paste it into Self Service.
help_ss_login_li1: Open Self Service from the monitor menu. If you are not logged in, enter your callsign and password (or use Login by IP when your hotspot is on the same public IP).
help_ss_login_li3: Login by IP is only available when the hotspot is already connected from your current public IP.
help_ss_device_instruc: Clear any local OPTIONS on the hotspot and set only PASS=your_password in Pi-Star or WPSD. The server supplies the OPTIONS line after you save in Self Service.
help_ss_img_pistar_alt: Pi-Star: Options and PASS fields in DMR Network
help_ss_img_wpsd_alt: WPSD: Options and PASS fields in DMR Gateway
help_static_dynamic_static_body: Listed in your OPTIONS as TS1= or TS2= (comma-separated TG numbers). They are always available on your hotspot and appear as fixed chips on the dashboard. Configure them in Self Service or in your hotspot OPTIONS field.
help_static_dynamic_dynamic_body: When you transmit to a talkgroup that is not in your static lists, the server opens a temporary bridge for your hotspot. These show as indigo chips on the dashboard. Since ADN server version 2.0.0, they can survive a hotspot reconnect.
help_static_dynamic_note: Network ACLs may block some TGs. Static lists define what your hotspot advertises; dynamic TGs are created when you actually key them.
help_options_manual_a1: If your network does not offer Self Service or you prefer not to use the dashboard, apply the OPTIONS line directly in your hotspot software.
help_options_manual_li1: Pi-Star / WPSD — paste the full OPTIONS line in the “Options” or “DMR Network Options” field (MMDVMHost / DMR Gateway).
help_options_manual_li2: Options Calculator — build the line in the menu, then copy and paste it into the hotspot Options field.
help_options_manual_img_pistar_alt: Pi-Star: Options field in DMR Network (MMDVMHost)
help_options_manual_img_wpsd_alt: WPSD: Options field in DMR Gateway
help_options_manual_calc: Build the line with the Options Calculator, copy it, and paste it into the hotspot Options field.
help_single_timer_single1_body: Only one talkgroup per timeslot is selected for receive at a time. While you are not transmitting, you can hear traffic from all static TGs (in OPTIONS) and any dynamic TGs. When you transmit on one TG, that becomes the only TG you receive on that slot until the TIMER expires, you key TG 4000, or you transmit on a different TG.
help_single_timer_single0_body: There is no receive exclusivity: when the hotspot is idle (not transmitting), it receives traffic from all configured static and dynamic TGs. Several dynamic talkgroups can coexist on the same slot; the dashboard may show multiple indigo chips until you clear them with TG 4000.
help_single_timer_timer_body: With SINGLE=1, how many minutes the selected dynamic TG stays active before the server removes it. In the Options Calculator, leave the timeout at 0 to omit TIMER and use the master default (DEFAULT_UA_TIMER). Do not write TIMER=0 in the OPTIONS line — the server treats that as no expiry, not the default.
help_single_timer_defaults: In the Options Calculator, “Server default” leaves SINGLE and TIMER to whatever your sysop configured on the master.
help_tg4000_a1: TG 4000 is not a talkgroup for normal QSOs. It is a reset command for your hotspot: key TG 4000 briefly to clear all dynamic (user-activated) bridges on all timeslots.
help_tg4000_li1: Does not remove talkgroups from your static TS1/TS2 lists.
help_tg4000_li2: Clears indigo dynamic chips on the dashboard for your hotspot.
help_tg4000_li3: Useful when you are stuck on an old dynamic TG or after an unexpected disconnect.
help_ta_a1: Talker Alias is a short text label (callsign, name, etc.) that some DMR radios can show on the display during a group call. It is carried inside the voice stream — not a talkgroup or service you dial.
help_ta_a2: This is not the same as the name on the ADN Monitor dashboard or Last Heard log. Those come from the subscriber database. Talker Alias is meant for your radio screen (OLED, MD380tools, some Hytera models).
help_ta_li1: Your radio firmware must support Talker Alias. Motorola MOTOTRBO added it from M2020.02 (2020) — without up-to-date firmware you will not see it on Motorola radios.
help_ta_li2: On Pi-Star / WPSD: keep DMR EmbeddedLCOnly off — when enabled, Talker Alias from the network is blocked.
help_ta_li3: Pi-Star DMR DumpTAData = on helps log-based tools show TA; your RF radio can still display TA either way.
help_ta_note: Whether Talker Alias appears on bridged calls depends on your network operator. It is not configured from Self Service or the Options line — contact your sysop if you use a compatible radio and never see it.
help_daprs_a1: D-APRS is the APRS gateway on the ADN network (position reports, messages, and related data over DMR). Your radio or DMR client must be set up to send APRS over DMR to the D-APRS talkgroup — not via the OPTIONS line on the server.
help_daprs_a2: The default D-APRS talkgroup is 900999, unless your network administrator has configured a different TG in the server bridges.
help_daprs_a3: D-APRS is optional: each ADN server may have it enabled or not. Some networks do not offer the service — if yours does not, you will not see a D-APRS entry under Linked Systems → Bridges (IP).
help_daprs_li1: On your radio, configure D-APRS / APRS-over-DMR to transmit to TG 900999 (or your network’s D-APRS TG if the administrator changed it).
help_daprs_li2: To confirm which TG is in use, open Linked Systems → Bridges (IP) and look for the D-APRS row and its TG column when the service is available.
help_echo_a1: Echo records your group voice transmission and plays it back to you — useful to test audio, levels, and connectivity. Transmit on the echo talkgroup and you should hear your own recording shortly after.
help_echo_a2: The default echo talkgroup is 9990, unless your network administrator has configured a different TG in the server bridges.
help_echo_a3: Echo is optional: each ADN server may have it enabled or not. Some networks do not offer the service — if yours does not, you will not see an ECHO entry under Linked Systems → Bridges (IP).
help_echo_li1: On your radio, select or key TG 9990 (or your network’s echo TG if the administrator changed it) and make a short test transmission.
help_echo_li2: To confirm which TG is in use, open Linked Systems → Bridges (IP) and look for the ECHO row and its TG column when the service is available.
help_faq_air_section: How ADN works on the air
help_air_ptt_q: What happens when I press PTT?
help_air_ptt_a1: When you press PTT, your radio sends your voice to your hotspot, which forwards it over the internet to the ADN server. The server checks that the talkgroup is free, then delivers your audio to every hotspot that has that talkgroup active. When you release PTT, you hear the other stations on the same talkgroup.
help_air_ptt_li1: You only need to select the talkgroup and press PTT — the server handles routing automatically.
help_air_ptt_li2: One short transmission is enough to activate a talkgroup you do not have in your static list.
help_air_ptt_li3: If the talkgroup is already in use, see the next question.
help_air_onecall_q: Can two people talk on the same talkgroup at once?
help_air_onecall_a1: No. Only one conversation per talkgroup may exist on the server at a time. If someone is already talking and you press PTT on the same talkgroup, the server does not let you interrupt them.
help_air_onecall_a2: However, if the talkgroup is busy and you do not have it in your static list, keying it briefly activates it as a dynamic talkgroup so you can listen to the ongoing conversation.
help_air_join_q: Do I miss the beginning of a call if I connect mid-QSO?
help_air_join_a1: No. When your hotspot connects and a talkgroup you listen to is already active, the server starts delivering the audio from that moment on. You do not need to wait for the next PTT to start hearing the conversation. You will miss the part that happened before you connected, but you join the live stream immediately.
help_air_slot_q: Do I need to worry about timeslots?
help_air_slot_a1: Usually no. Your hotspot and the server coordinate the timeslot automatically. You receive a talkgroup on the slot where it is configured in your OPTIONS, regardless of the slot used by the station that is transmitting. DMR has two timeslots (TS1 and TS2); they are independent, so you can talk on TS1 while you listen on TS2 if your hotspot is full-duplex.
help_etiquette_intro: These habits keep conversations clear and pleasant for everyone on the network:
help_etiquette_li1: Listen before you talk — wait a second after the previous station finishes to make sure no one else is still speaking.
help_etiquette_li2: Identify yourself — say your callsign when you start and when you finish. It is required by regulations and helps others know who is talking.
help_etiquette_li3: Leave short pauses (2–3 seconds) between turns so other stations can join the conversation and to avoid cutting off the first syllable.
help_etiquette_li4: Do not key PTT too quickly after releasing it; a short pause lets the server process the change cleanly.
help_etiquette_li5: When you finish with a dynamic talkgroup, key TG 4000 to clear it — this frees resources and stops traffic you no longer want to hear.
help_etiquette_summary: In short: select the TG, press PTT to talk, release to listen, and key 4000 when you want to “hang up”. The server does everything else.
console_desc: Start/end of call messages (GROUP VOICE START/END).
ss_intro: Here you can manage your hotspot preferences and static talk groups.
ss_calc_hint: Need help building your options line? Use the
ss_purge_dynamic_hint: Clears user-activated talkgroups (same as transmitting to TG 4000).
ss_purge_dynamic_confirm: Remove user-activated TGs for this hotspot? (equivalent to keying TG 4000)
ss_purge_dynamic_ok: Reload in progress — dynamic TGs will clear within about 10 seconds.
active_qso_none: No active QSO at the moment.
tbl_filter_tg_hint: Shows all TGs whose number contains this (e.g. 730 = Chile)
tbl_filter_name_hint: Shows TGs whose name contains this text
sslog_manage_options_tg: Self Service lets you manage your device's Options line and static talk groups.
sslog_instruc: In order to manage your device with Self Service, you may need to remove all options in your device's software and set your password using PASS=your_password.
lnksys_tg_legend: Indigo chips: user-activated (dynamic) TG while the UA bridge is active.
toptg_no_data: No TG count data yet. Data is saved when voice QSOs end (duration > 5s).
